message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    There is a shortage of chemical reagents for severe acute respiratory syndrome - coronavirus 2 (
                    <jats:italic>SARS-CoV-2</jats:italic>
                    ) diagnosis and a surge of
                    <jats:italic>SARS-CoV-2</jats:italic>
                    cases, especially in limited-resource settings. Therefore, the combination of an optimal assay kit is necessary.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We compared the ability to screen
                    <jats:italic>SARS-CoV-2</jats:italic>
                    among three primer-probe sets in two different master mixes, Invitrogen™ SuperScript™ III One-Step RT-PCR and LightCycler Multiplex RNA Virus Master.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The assay with TIB-Molbiol, IDT, and Phu Sa sets for LightCycler Multiplex RNA Virus Master or Invitrogen™ SuperScript™ III One-Step RT-PCR showed positive results from a single reaction of triplicate in the three days of 4.8 copies per reaction. R-squared and amplification efficiency were 0.97 and ranged from 107 to 108%, respectively.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Our findings indicated that TIB-Molbiol, IDT, and Phu Sa primer-probe sets could be beneficial for the laboratory screening of
                    <jats:italic>SARS-CoV-2</jats:italic>
                    by RT-qPCR assay of E gene. There is a need to consider the combination of these reagent sets as a new strategy to increase the testing capacity of screening programs for COVID-19.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>

reference.article-title: The effect of primer-template mismatches on the detection and quantification of nucleic acids using the 5' nuclease assay
